agent1_snippets.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Issues of Concern
agent1_snippets.content: One of the technical complications reported in the YTZ-P reconstructions is chipping or delamination of the veneering porcelain. The core-veneer interface is a critical factor in the success of layered restorations. The contributing factors for the fracture may include weakness of the veneer material or the core/veneer bond, stresses and distortion due to the veneering process, unsupported veneering porcelain and configuration of the core and veneer, residual stresses arising from a coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch, and rapid cooling rates after heat treatment. The use of high-speed polishing rotary instruments is contraindicated for clinical use because of nickel contamination, phase changes in zirconia, and the resultant surface roughness.

agent1_snippets.title: Le Fort Fractures -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: A combination of blows with a wooden club and a metal rod, stomping on the face, crushing with a vise, and "hurling" heads at the edge of a table was employed by his aides, after which the skulls were boiled to facilitate removal of the soft tissue for subsequent osseous examination. [1] [2] Despite the lack of consistency in the experimental protocol to force, orientation, and mechanism of injury, Le Fort's work described many patterns in which fractures most commonly occur along the “lines of least resistance” ( linea minoris resistentiae ) that he identified (see Images . Facial Great Lines of Weakness, Profile View and Facial Great Lines of Weakness, Front View). [2] These patterns are now classified into 3 types based on their orientation: horizontal (type I), pyramidal (type II), and transverse (type III). All of these injuries separate the upper jaw from the skull and, by definition, involve fractures of the pterygoid plates of the sphenoid bone. The severe pattern fractures (pyramidal and transverse) occur more superiorly than the horizontal fracture, separating more of the facial skeleton from the neurocranium. These fractures are typically the result of greater forces applied to the central and upper midface.

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Retained dental roots may present with one or many indications for removal. Some of the most common indications for the extraction of root fragments are: The presence of acute or chronic infection Partial or complete fracture of the crown Future dental implant placement Pain Vertical root fractures Periodontal disease Caries Presence of pathology Future prosthetic restoration of the site with a fixed or removable prosthesis Preservation of vital adjacent teeth and structures Patient esthetic concerns

agent1_snippets.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Partial pulpotomy
agent1_snippets.content: Partial pulpotomy involves amputating the pulp 2 mm below the exposure site, where the tissue is presumed to be healthy. [16] This technique is the treatment of choice for young teeth with open apices when pulp capping is contraindicated, such as when excessive time has elapsed since the trauma or when the exposure diameter is too large. Partial pulpotomy serves as a provisional yet long-term approach, allowing the tooth to remain vital for as long as possible. However, root canal treatment followed by appropriate restoration may eventually be required. Restorative options include freehand or indirect composite resin restoration, fragment reattachment if available, and ceramic restoration.

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Stainless steel crowns are the most commonly used restorative option for repairing and preserving the remaining tissue of severely damaged and decayed teeth (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown). They were introduced into pediatric dentistry in 1947, first described by Engel, and then popularised by Humphrey in 1950. Stainless steel crowns have outperformed other materials, such as amalgam and composite, in terms of durability and longevity for more than a half-century. In fact, no restorative material has provided the benefits of low cost, reliability, and durability when interim full-coronal coverage is required. [3] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Primary teeth are relatively at a larger risk of caries than permanent teeth. They also have larger pulp with prominent pulp horns, which places exact demands on cavity design. Hence, a full-coverage restoration is required. Although stainless steel crowns have an aesthetic disadvantage, they are still the treatment of choice for the following reasons: Durability Efficiency Longevity Cost-effectiveness Reliability Even though other tooth-colored restorations are available in the market, they are costly or require more tooth reduction, increasing the chances of pulp exposure. agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation
agent1_snippets.content: A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is the preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Surgically Applicable Machining Properties
agent1_snippets.content: Early intramedullary nails rod were made of stainless steel, including tri-flange nails, among others. [6] Many early intramedullary nails were successful, but some went on to atrophic non-union due to the marked stiffness of these nails. In an attempt to improve these complications, some nails where "slotted" or given a horseshoe-like cross-section to make them less rigid. Fracture care with hardware has been evolving as we gain more experience.  In the 1970s through the 1990s, rigid constructs (stainless steel) were preferred.  In the past 20 years, there has been a shift away from "complete rigidity." Modern fracture fixation theories favor a less rigid construct to enhance bone healing.

agent1_snippets.content: The treatment aims at preserving the pulp by partial pulpotomy. agent1_snippets.content: The primary challenge in the restorative management of crown-root fractures is ensuring restorability, as subgingival margins often necessitate additional treatment to expose more tooth structure and establish a ferrule. agent1_snippets.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: The restorations are processed either by soft machining of pre-sintered blanks or by hard machining of fully sintered blocks. The mechanical properties of 3Y-TZP are strongly affected by its grain size. The mechanical properties of zirconia have been reported to be higher than other ceramics for dental applications. Fracture resistance of 6–10 MPa/m1/2, a flexural strength of 900–1200 MPa, and a compression resistance of 2000 MPa have been reported. Zirconia restorations bear an average load of 755 N. Zirconia yields higher fracture loads than alumina or lithium disilicate. Zirconia ceramics yield superior wear behavior and lower antagonistic wear compared to porcelain.

agent1_snippets.title: Elbow Fractures Overview -- Treatment / Management -- Interventions for Supracondylar Fractures
agent1_snippets.content: Closed reduction with percutaneous pinning is a standard technique in managing displaced fractures. Bone fragments are realigned through closed reduction and stabilized with 2 lateral metal pins. Three pins may be required in the presence of severe displacement or a floating distal segment. The fixation is reinforced with a splint or cast, and both the pins and immobilization device are removed once adequate healing is observed in the ensuing weeks.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. A specialized form, **internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction**, is also included. The “internal fixation of fracture” concept itself is a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density SMQ, reinforcing the association between osteoporotic bone and the need for fixation even when reduction is not performed. In the same SMQ, the concepts **إعاضة رأس الفخذ** (hip‑head prosthesis) and **كسور متعددة** (multiple fractures) are identified, underscoring that patients with reduced bone quality are prone to multiple fractures and may require prosthetic reconstruction of the femoral head. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
agent2_snippets.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.

This procedure, together with other fracture‑related interventions such as hip‑head prosthetic replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and the broader category of “other fractures,” is classified within the osteoporosis/low bone mass syndrome (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام SMQ). Consequently, these surgical treatments are particularly relevant for patients with reduced bone density, highlighting the close relationship between osteoporotic bone disease and the need for internal fixation or prosthetic reconstruction of various skeletal sites.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
agent2_snippets.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

agent2_snippets.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
agent2_snippets.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
agent2_snippets.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.
